preamble: The development of a voiced dental stop [d] into a voiced postalveolar affricate [dʒ] is a classic example of dental palatalization. This often occurs via yod-coalescence (d + j > dʒ), as seen in English 'soldier' or Latin 'diurnum' > Italian 'giorno'. The process involves fronting and affrication triggered by a following high front vowel or palatal glide.

examples.note: Historical yod-coalescence where /d/ and /j/ merge into [dʒ].
Latin /dj/ sequence resulting in Italian affricate.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Philology.
